title: Protecting Adolescents From Harm
abstract:
Letters
Protecting Adolescents From Harm
To the Editor.—The study by Dr Resnick and colleagues,1
particularly their findings regarding parent-family connect-
edness as a protective factor against emotional distress, is
timely, given the destructive waves that are eroding the in-
stitutionofthefamilyintheUnitedStates.Theirfindingshave
importantsocialpolicyimplications,giventheever-increasing
rates of marital dissolution (marriages that end in divorce are
estimated at over 60%), single-parent families (over 30%),
births to unwed mothers (over 30%), and the unprecedented
increasing proportion of mothers in paid employment with
toddlers and children younger than 6 years (over 60%).2
Resnick and colleagues could have strengthened their conclu-
sions by relating their findings to these current social trends.
Because of the “political correctness” mentality that is pre-
vailing in academia,3 health professionals seem to refrain from
open discussion of their findings’ implications for social issues,
such as parental unavailability to their children, family disin-
tegration, and other onrushing social tides that can cause pub-
lic health problems.
Despite the stated shortcoming, the consistencies between
findings by Resnick and colleagues1 and other empirical stud-
ies lend more credence to their conclusions. In a prospective
study,itwasfoundthatparentalwarmthandaffectioninchild-
hood was associated with psychological well-being and social
accomplishment35yearslater.4Inastudyofmedicalstudents,
maternal availability prior to the child’s fifth birthday pre-
dicted healthier personality in adulthood.3 In another study of
physicians, perception of an affectionate relationship with the
motherinchildhoodwasassociatedwithhigherratingsofclini-
cal competence in interpersonal skills and attitudes.5 Such an
association was not found for physicians’ perception of pater-
nalwarmthandaffection.Inarecentstudywithalargesample
of medical students, reported satisfaction with early relation-
ships with the mother was linked to fewer experiences of lone-
liness, less depression, less anxiety, more resiliency, higher
self-esteem, and better peer relationships in adulthood.6 Such
a link was not found for satisfaction with early relationships
with the father. These findings, coupled with the changing
status of the parents in the new transitional family in the
United States, make it desirable to single out the relative
contributions of each parent to prosocial behavior and to emo-
tional distress, rather than studying the combined parental
contribution.
With regard to the increasing number of mothers in paid
employment, expansion of alternative child care enterprise,
and a lack of parental leave policies in the United States to
enableparentstobewiththeirinfants,thefindingsbyResnick
andcolleagues1haveimplicationsforformulationofsocialpoli-
ciestofacilitateafullandharmoniousdevelopmentofourmost
valuable resources of tomorrow’s society.
Mohammadreza Hojat, PhD
Jefferson Medical College
Philadelphia, Pa
1. Resnick MD, Bearman PS, Blum RW, et al. Protecting adolescents from harm:
findings from the National Longitudinal Study on Adolescent Health. JAMA. 1997;
278:823-832.
2. Hojat M. Developmental pathways to violence: a psychodynamic paradigm. Peace
Psychol Rev. 1995;1:177-196.
3. Hojat M. Perception of maternal availability in childhood and selected psychologi-
cal characteristics in adulthood. Genet Soc Gen Psychol Monogr. 1996;122:425-445.
4. Franz CE, McClelland DC, Weinberger J. Childhood antecedents of conventional
social accomplishment in midlife adults: a 36-year prospective study. J Pers Soc Psy-
chol. 1991;60:586-595.
5. Hojat M, Glaser KM, Veloski JJ. Associations between selected psychological
attributes and ratings of physician competence. Acad Med. 1996;71(suppl):5103-5105.
6. Hojat M. Satisfaction with early relationships with the father and the mother and
psychosocial attributes in adulthood: which parent contributes more? J Gen Psychol.
In press.
In Reply.—The National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent
Health holds tremendous promise for having an impact on pro-
grams, policies, and practices affecting young people. Dr Hojat
correctlyemphasizestheimportanceofconductinganalysesthat
separatelyassessthecontributionsofmothersandfatherstothe
health and well-being of youth, just as separate analyses across
racial, ethnic, social class, sex, and geographic groups of adoles-
cents will yield powerful insights about how to better focus our
efforts in health promotion and protection.
However, our article from the Add Health data set delib-
erately did not seek to focus on the special needs of particular
groups, nor the separate effects of mothers and fathers on
adolescents. Rather, our analysis sought to highlight salient,
cross-cutting protective factors at the individual, family, and
school levels that affect the major risky behaviors of adoles-
cents, controlling for the effects of family structure, poverty
status, sex, race, and ethnicity, to help illuminate factors with
potential universal applications. With the release of Add
Health as a public use data set, we are solidly assured that
there will be multiple analyses that focus on unique aspects of
health and health threats among particular groups of adoles-
cents, just as there will be analyses that illuminate the sepa-
rate and distinct contributions of mothers and fathers to ado-
lescent health outcomes. It is premature to be concerned that
our initial analyses did not include this focus on the particular
rather than the universal.
As researchers seek to disaggregate the effects of mothers
and fathers on adolescents’ health, it is vital that they are
guided by an expansive understanding of relevant literature,
which suggests the importance of fathers in terms of short-
and long-term outcomes among young people. Recent analy-
ses of the Add Health data set by Kathleen Mullin Harris,
PhD, a member of the University of North Carolina, Chapel
Hill,team,revealthataftercontrollingfortheeffectsofmother
involvement, active father involvement in the lives of adoles-
cent sons and daughters (whether the father is resident or
nonresident) produces lower levels of adolescent health risk
behaviors.1 Similarly, the work of Parke and colleagues2 has
demonstrated the positive impact of father involvement on
adolescent behavior. Longitudinal studies by Bronstein et al3
havealsodocumentedthesalutaryeffectsofsupportiveaware
parenting by fathers (as well as mothers).
Edited by Margaret A. Winker, MD, Senior Editor, and Phil B. Fontanarosa,
MD, Senior Editor.
Guidelines for Letters
Letters will be published at the discretion of the editors as space per-
mits and are subject to editing and abridgment. Letters will be
considered if they are typewritten double-spaced and do not exceed
500 words of text and 5 references. Please include a word count.
Letters discussing a recent JAMA article should be received within 4
weeks of the article’s publication. Letters must not duplicate
other material published or submitted for publication. A signed state-
ment for authorship criteria and responsibility, financial disclosure,
copyright transfer, and acknowledgment is essential for publica-
tion. Letters not meeting these specifications are generally not
considered.Letterswillnotbereturnedunlessspecificallyrequested.
Also see JAMA Instructions for Authors (January 7, 1998). Letters
maybesubmittedbysurfacemail:LettersEditor,JAMA,515NState
St, Chicago, IL 60610; e-mail: JAMA-letters@ama-assn.org; or fax
(please also send a hard copy via surface mail): (312) 464-5824.
JAMA, February 4, 1998—Vol 279, No. 5
Letters

 Reflecting Harris’ work noted above, it is encouraging that
the material prepared for the recent NICHD (National Insti-
tute of Child Health and Human Development) Conference on
Fathering and Male Fertility: Improving Data and Research
further demonstrates the relevance and importance of fathers
to the health and well-being of their children, including ado-
lescents.4 Based on this growing body of evidence, one may
expect that forthcoming analyses from Add Health that sepa-
rately assess the impact of parents on their adolescents will
point to the need for family-friendly programs and policies
that enhance the capacity of both fathers and mothers to suc-
cessfully socialize, nurture, and otherwise guide the develop-
ment of their children.
Michael D. Resnick, PhD
University of Minnesota
Minneapolis
1. Harris KM. The impact of family structure and father involvement on risk behav-
ior among adolescents. Paper presented at: National Institute of Child Health and
Human Development Conference on Father Involvement; October 10-11, 1996;
Bethesda, Md.
2. Parke RD, Ladd GW, eds. Family-Peer Relationships: Modes of Linkage. Hins-
dale, NJ: Erlbaum; 1992.
3. Bronstein P, Duncan P, D’Ari A, et al. Family and parenting behaviors predicting
middle school adjustment: a longitudinal study. Fam Relations. 1996;45:415-426.
4. Marsiglio W, Day R, Braver S, Evans VJ, Lamb M, Peters E. Social fatherhood and
paternal involvement: conceptual, data, and policymaking issues. Paper presented at:
NICHD-sponsored Conference on Fathering and Male Fertility: Improving Data and
Research; March 13-14, 1997; Bethesda, Md.
Increasing Incidence of AIDS Among Women
To the Editor.—In the article on the acquired immunodefi-
ciency syndrome (AIDS) among women in the United States,1
Drs Wortley and Fleming refer to preliminary findings of a
study to validate cases classified as heterosexual by the Cen-
ters for Disease Control and Prevention human immunodefi-
ciency virus (CDC HIV) surveillance program. Thesedataare
of critical importance in determining trends in AIDS inci-
dence.However,theabstract2referencedinthearticlemerely
provides a 3-line description of the study objectives.
ThereaderalsoshouldbeawarethattheCDCHIVexposure
classificationdoesnotincludeacategoryforheterosexualtrans-
mission in which neither party can be classified as belonging to
another recognized risk group. These cases should become in-
creasingly common as the virus extends even deeper into the
heterosexualpopulationandneedtobefollowedinacategoryof
their own. Currently, these cases are simply classified as “risk
not identified.” This approach represents an increasingly im-
portant bias in determining AIDS trends, let alone estimating
HIV trends based on these data. Reliance on CDC seropreva-
lence data to support conclusions drawn from the AIDS data at
a time when incidence rates are changing in the transmission
groups can be misleading. Strickler et al3 have reviewed the
problems involved in interpreting this type of data when major
changes are occurring in the demographic and behavioral char-
acteristics of the epidemic. Until an accurate method to classify
heterosexual transmission of HIV is available and a national
HIVreportingsystemisinplace,greatcareshouldbeexercised
in making inferences drawn from the available data (including
seroprevalence studies) when those data are used to make pre-
dictions on the future course of the epidemic.
W. Shepherd Smith
Fred J. Payne, MD
Americans for a Sound AIDS/HIV Policy
Washington, DC
1. Wortley PM, Fleming PL. AIDS in women in the United States: recent trends.
JAMA. 1997;278:911-916.
2. Klevens RM, Fleming PL, Neal JJ, Li J, and the MTV Study Group. Does misclas-
sification of HIV exposure impact AIDS trends among heterosexuals in the United
States? In: Proceedings of the XI International Conference on AIDS; July 7-12, 1996;
Vancouver, British Columbia. Abstract Th.C.340.
3. Strickler H, Hoover DR, Dersimonian R. Problems in interpreting HIV sentinel
seroprevalence studies. AEP. 1995;5:447-454.
To the Editor.—Drs Wortley and Fleming1 recently reported
increasesinAIDSamongwomenintheUnitedStates,account-
ing for 19% of all cases in 1995, with incidence rates exceeding
20 per 100 000 in Northeastern areas, in blacks and Hispanics.
We reported steady and substantial increases in AIDS among
European women from 1985 to 1994, reaching rates between 4
and 9 per 100 000 and accounting for 15% to 20% of cases in
southern Europe (ie, France, Spain, and Italy).2,3
WeupdatedAIDStrendsinEuropeanwomenaged13years
or older, using data from the European Non-Aggregate AIDS
Data Set, adjusted for reporting delay in each country.3 Less
than2%ofEuropeanAIDScaseshadoccurredincountriesnot
included in the current report. Rates were age adjusted on the
basis of the world standard population. The use of the US
population as a standard, as in the article by Wortley et al,
yielded similar rates (not shown).
The number of AIDS cases and incidence rates in 1991
through 1995 in 4 European geographic areas and all of Europe
are shown in the Table. The incidence of AIDS among women
increased by 100% in southern Europe, by 60% in central Eu-
rope, by 113% in northern Europe, and by 367% in eastern
Europe. In southern Europe, most cases (54%) were attributed
to injection drug use (IDU), whereas in northern Europe, the
majority(71%)wasattributedtoheterosexualcontact.In1995,
correspondingincidenceratesamongmenwereapproximately
4-fold greater (ie, 26.2 in southern Europe, 5.3 in central Eu-
rope, and 6.5 in northern Europe), except in eastern Europe.
Upward trends in AIDS among women were steadier and
larger at ages 25 to 49 years in southern Europe (Figure) than
throughout all of Europe. When trends in the Figure were
replicated separately for IDU and heterosexual AIDS cases,
similar patterns emerged (data not shown).
Comparison of incidence rates in Europe and the United
Statesishamperedbecausethe1993AIDScasedefinitionbased
on severe immunosuppression has not been adopted in Europe4
and by differences in allowance for reporting delay.5 Further-
